abstract:
<jats:p>
                    Each month, a fraction of UK property leases are extended by 90 years or more. We construct a new dataset using thousands of these natural experiments since 2000 and estimate the expected long-term housing yield, y
                    <jats:sup>*</jats:sup>
                    . After remaining steady at around 5 percent, y
                    <jats:sup>*</jats:sup>
                    starts to decline when the Great Recession hits and reaches a low of 2.7 percent in 2024. The decline is steeper in inelastic markets, while y
                    <jats:sup>*</jats:sup>
                    remains higher in regions more exposed to long-run climate risk. Our estimate of y
                    <jats:sup>*</jats:sup>
                    is updated in real time using public data. (JEL E32, G12, Q54, R31, R38)
                  </jats:p>
title: Dynamics of the Long-Term Housing Yield: Evidence from Natural Experiments
